title: Contains Duplicate
description: Given an integer array nums, your task is to determine whether any value appears at least twice in the array. If any duplicate value exists, return true. If every element in the array is completely distinct (i.e., no two elements are equal), return false. This is one of the most fundamental hashing problems because it directly demonstrates the core use case of a HashSet: O(1) average-time membership lookup. A naive brute-force approach compares every pair of elements in O(n^2), but using a HashSet allows you to check duplicates in a single O(n) pass. As you iterate, you ask: have I seen this number before? If yes, it's a duplicate. If no, add it to the set and move on.
hints: Use a HashSet to track seen elements.
If you try to insert a duplicate, return true.
followUp: Can you solve it without extra space using sorting?

title: Two Sum
description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to target. You may assume that each input has exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. This is arguably the most famous hashing problem. The brute force checks every pair in O(n^2), but a HashMap reduces it to O(n). The key insight is: for each number x at index i, you don't need to scan the rest of the array. Instead, compute the complement (target - x) and check if that complement was already stored in the map. If it was, you found your pair immediately. If not, store x with its index and continue. This complement-lookup pattern is the backbone of many harder hashing problems like 4Sum II and Subarray Sum Equals K.
hints: Store each number and its index in a HashMap.
For each element, check if target - element exists in the map.
followUp: What if the array is sorted?

title: Valid Anagram
description: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s, and false otherwise. An anagram is a word formed by rearranging the letters of another word using all original letters exactly once. For example, 'listen' and 'silent' are anagrams. The hashing approach uses a frequency count map: count how many times each character appears in s, then subtract counts for each character in t. If the map is all zeros at the end, the strings are anagrams. Alternatively, you can use two separate frequency maps and compare them. This problem teaches the frequency-count pattern which is reused in Group Anagrams, Find All Anagrams in a String, and Minimum Window Substring.

examples.explanation: Both strings have the same character frequencies.

constraints: s and t consist of lowercase English letters.
hints: Count character frequencies using a HashMap.
Compare both frequency maps.
relatedProblems: Find All Anagrams in a String
followUp: What if inputs contain Unicode characters?

title: Design HashMap
description: Design a HashMap from scratch without using any built-in hash table libraries. You must implement the MyHashMap class supporting three operations: put(key, value) which inserts or updates a key-value pair, get(key) which returns the value for a key or -1 if not found, and remove(key) which deletes the key-value pair if it exists. This problem forces you to understand what actually happens under the hood of every HashMap you use. You must choose a hash function (typically key % tableSize), handle collisions using separate chaining (array of linked lists) or open addressing, and manage edge cases like updating existing keys. Building this from scratch is the best way to truly internalize why hash tables give O(1) average performance and what causes worst-case O(n) behavior.

examples.input: put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), get(3), put(2,1), get(2), remove(2), get(2)

constraints: At most 10^4 calls to put, get, and remove.
hints: Use an array of linked lists (chaining).
Hash function: key % tableSize.
followUp: How would you handle resizing?

title: Design HashSet
description: Design a HashSet from scratch without using any built-in hash set libraries. You must implement the MyHashSet class supporting three operations: add(key) which inserts the key if not already present, contains(key) which returns true if the key exists, and remove(key) which removes the key if it exists. Similar to Design HashMap but simpler since you only store keys without values. This problem is the gateway to understanding the internal mechanics of sets. You will implement a hash function, choose a collision resolution strategy, and reason about load factor. The typical implementation uses an array of buckets where each bucket is a linked list, giving O(1) average case for all three operations.

examples.input: add(1), add(2), contains(1), contains(3), add(2), contains(2), remove(2), contains(2)

constraints: At most 10^4 calls to add, remove, and contains.
hints: Use an array of buckets with linked lists.
Use modulo as the hash function.
followUp: Compare your design with Java's HashSet implementation.

title: Jewels and Stones
description: You are given two strings: jewels, which represents the types of stones that are jewels, and stones, which represents the stones you have. Each character in stones represents a stone you own, and you want to know how many of your stones are also jewels. Letters are case-sensitive, so 'a' is a different type from 'A'. This is one of the simplest practical applications of a HashSet. Instead of doing a nested loop (O(j * s)), you insert all jewel types into a HashSet in O(j) time, then iterate through each stone and check membership in O(1) per lookup. The total time is O(j + s). While simple, this problem is a perfect first example of trading space for time using hashing.

examples.explanation: a, A, A are jewels in stones.

hints: Store jewels in a HashSet.
Count how many stones are in the set.
followUp: What if the input is a stream?

title: Maximum Number of Balloons
description: Given a string text, you need to find the maximum number of times you can form the word 'balloon' using the characters from text. Each character from text can be used at most once. The word 'balloon' requires: 1x 'b', 1x 'a', 2x 'l', 2x 'o', 1x 'n'. This problem is a direct application of the frequency-count hashing pattern. Count the occurrences of each required letter in text using a HashMap, then divide the counts appropriately (divide by 2 for 'l' and 'o' since they appear twice in 'balloon'), and return the minimum across all required characters. This teaches you how to generalize frequency matching for any target word.
constraints: text consists of lower case English letters only.
hints: Count frequency of b, a, l, o, n in text.
l and o need to appear twice per balloon.
relatedProblems: Find All Anagrams in a String
followUp: Can you generalize for any target word?

title: Word Pattern
description: Given a pattern string (e.g., 'abba') and a string s containing space-separated words, determine if s follows the same pattern. This means there must be a bijection (one-to-one and onto mapping) between each character in pattern and each word in s. For example, if 'a' maps to 'dog', every 'a' must map to 'dog' and no other letter maps to 'dog'. This problem requires checking a bijection in both directions simultaneously. Use two HashMaps: one from pattern character to word, and another from word to pattern character. At each position, if the current character already maps to a different word (or vice versa), return false. This bidirectional mapping technique also appears in Isomorphic Strings and is critical in any encoding/decoding problem.
constraints: s contains only lowercase English words separated by a single space.
hints: Use two HashMaps: one for pattern->word and one for word->pattern.
Check bijection in both directions.
followUp: What about Word Pattern II?

title: Isomorphic Strings
description: Given two strings s and t, determine if they are isomorphic. Two strings are isomorphic if the characters in s can be replaced to get t, with a consistent one-to-one mapping where no two characters map to the same character but each character maps to exactly one character. For example, 'egg' and 'add' are isomorphic because 'e' maps to 'a' and 'g' maps to 'd'. But 'foo' and 'bar' are not, because 'o' would need to map to both 'a' and 'r'. The solution uses two HashMaps for bidirectional verification at every character position. This is structurally identical to Word Pattern but operates at the character level instead of the word level. Mastering this teaches you the general bijection-check template.

examples.explanation: o maps to both a and r.

hints: Use two HashMaps for bidirectional mapping.
Check for consistency at each character pair.
followUp: Can you solve using only one map?

title: Ransom Note
description: Given two strings ransomNote and magazine, return true if ransomNote can be constructed by using the letters from magazine. Each letter in magazine can only be used once. Imagine cutting letters from a magazine to form a ransom note — you need enough copies of each letter available. The solution is a direct application of frequency-count hashing: build a frequency map of all characters in magazine, then for each character in ransomNote, decrement its count. If any count drops below zero, the magazine doesn't have enough of that letter, so return false. This problem reinforces the idea that a HashMap can represent a multiset (a set allowing duplicate entries), which is a fundamental data structure pattern.
hints: Count letter frequencies in magazine.
Check if ransomNote can be satisfied by those counts.
followUp: What if each letter can only be used once?

title: Intersection of Two Arrays
description: Given two integer arrays nums1 and nums2, return an array of their intersection — the elements that appear in both arrays. Each element in the result must be unique (even if it appears multiple times in both arrays). This problem illustrates set intersection using hashing. Insert all elements of nums1 into a HashSet. Then iterate through nums2, and for each element that exists in the HashSet, add it to the result set (using another set ensures uniqueness). The result set becomes your answer. This is a clean O(n + m) solution compared to the O(n * m) naive approach. The intersection pattern appears in many harder problems involving two data sources.
hints: Convert nums1 to a HashSet.
Check each element in nums2 against the set.
followUp: What if the given array is already sorted?

title: Group Anagrams
description: Given an array of strings strs, group all strings that are anagrams of each other together and return the groups in any order. Two strings are anagrams if one can be formed by rearranging the letters of the other. The key insight is finding a canonical key that all anagrams share. The most common approach is to sort each string alphabetically — all anagrams produce the same sorted string (e.g., 'eat', 'tea', 'ate' all sort to 'aet'). Use a HashMap where the key is the sorted string and the value is the list of original strings that hash to it. An alternative O(n*k) key (avoiding sorting) uses a tuple of 26 character counts as the key. This problem teaches you how to design smart hash keys to group related items.
hints: Sort each string to create a canonical key.
Use a HashMap with sorted string as key and list of anagrams as value.
relatedProblems: Find All Anagrams in a String
followUp: Can you use character frequency as the key instead of sorting?

title: Top K Frequent Elements
description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements. You may return the answer in any order. The guaranteed constraint is that the answer is unique. Step one is always the same: build a frequency HashMap by counting occurrences of each element. Step two is where the interesting choices happen. Option A (O(n log k)): use a min-heap of size k; iterate through the frequency map and maintain only the top k elements. Option B (O(n)) — bucket sort: create an array of n+1 buckets where bucket[i] holds all elements that appear exactly i times, then scan from the end to collect the top k. The bucket sort approach is optimal and demonstrates how hashing can completely replace comparison-based sorting in specific scenarios.
constraints: k is in the range [1, number of unique elements]
hints: Count frequency with a HashMap.
Use a min-heap of size k or bucket sort by frequency.
relatedProblems: Kth Largest Element in an Array
followUp: Can you solve it in O(n) time using bucket sort?

title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
description: Given an unsorted array of integers nums, return the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence (e.g., [1,2,3,4]). The algorithm must run in O(n) time, which rules out sorting. The HashSet-based solution is elegant: insert all numbers into a HashSet for O(1) lookups, then for each number n, check if n-1 exists in the set. If it does NOT, then n is the start of a new sequence. From that start, keep incrementing and checking (n+1, n+2, ...) until the next number isn't in the set, counting the length. By only starting sequences at their true beginning, every number is visited at most twice total across all sequences, guaranteeing O(n). This problem shows how a HashSet can replace sorting when you only need membership queries.
hints: Store all numbers in a HashSet.
Only start counting from a number if num-1 is NOT in the set.
followUp: What if the array is already sorted?

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
description: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring that contains no repeating characters. A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within the string. This is the classic sliding window + hashing problem. Maintain a window with two pointers (left and right) and a HashSet (or HashMap) tracking the characters currently inside the window. Expand the right pointer to include new characters. When a duplicate is detected, shrink the window from the left until the duplicate is removed. The HashMap variant (storing character -> last seen index) allows jumping the left pointer directly to the position after the last occurrence of the duplicate, making the inner loop implicit. At each step, update the maximum window size. This problem is foundational for all sliding window problems.
constraints: s consists of English letters, digits, symbols and spaces.
hints: Use a sliding window with a HashSet.
Move the left pointer when a duplicate is found.

youtubeVideos.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters - NeetCode

relatedProblems: Longest Substring with At Most Two Distinct Characters
followUp: Can you use a HashMap storing indices to optimize the left pointer movement?

title: 4Sum II
description: Given four integer arrays nums1, nums2, nums3, and nums4, each of length n, count the number of tuples (i, j, k, l) such that nums1[i] + nums2[j] + nums3[k] + nums4[l] equals zero. The brute force is O(n^4), but hashing brings it to O(n^2). The trick is 'divide and conquer on pairs': compute every possible sum of nums1[i] + nums2[j] and store each sum along with how many times it occurs in a HashMap. Then iterate over every pair from nums3 and nums4, and for each sum S = nums3[k] + nums4[l], check if (-S) exists in the HashMap. If it does, add its count to the result. This problem elegantly demonstrates how to reduce a 4-variable problem to two 2-variable problems using a HashMap as a lookup bridge.
hints: Split into two pairs: count all sums of (nums1[i]+nums2[j]) in a HashMap.
For each sum in (nums3+nums4), check if its negation exists in the map.
followUp: How would you extend this to k arrays?

title: Find All Anagrams in a String
description: Given two strings s and p, return a list of all the starting indices in s where a substring of length p.length() is an anagram of p. The order of results does not matter. This combines two concepts: the sliding window (fixed-size window of length p) and frequency-count hashing. Maintain a frequency map for p and a running frequency map for the current window in s. Slide the window one character at a time: add the new right character and remove the leftmost character. After each slide, compare the two maps — if they are equal, the current window is an anagram. To avoid comparing full maps at every step (which is O(26) but still adds up), you can maintain a counter of how many characters are 'satisfied', updating it only when a count crosses the threshold.
constraints: s and p consist of lowercase English letters.
hints: Use a fixed-size sliding window of length p.
Compare character frequency maps.

youtubeVideos.title: Find All Anagrams in a String - NeetCode

followUp: Can you use a difference counter to avoid comparing two maps?

title: Subarray Sum Equals K
description: Given an array of integers nums (which may include negative numbers) and an integer k, return the total number of contiguous subarrays whose sum equals k. This problem cannot use a sliding window because the array may contain negative numbers (shrinking the window doesn't guarantee decreasing the sum). Instead, use prefix sums with a HashMap. Define prefixSum[i] as the sum of nums[0..i]. A subarray nums[j..i] has sum k if and only if prefixSum[i] - prefixSum[j-1] = k, i.e., prefixSum[j-1] = prefixSum[i] - k. As you iterate and compute prefix sums, store each prefix sum's frequency in a HashMap. For each new prefix sum S, look up how many times S-k has appeared before — each occurrence corresponds to a valid subarray ending here. Initialize the map with {0: 1} to handle subarrays starting from index 0.
hints: Use prefix sum. For each prefix sum S, check if S-k was seen before.
Store prefix sum frequencies in a HashMap.
followUp: What if all numbers are positive? Can you use sliding window?

title: Daily Temperatures
description: Given an array of daily temperatures, for each day return the number of days you need to wait until a warmer temperature. If there is no future day with a higher temperature, return 0 for that day. While not a pure hashing problem, it uses a HashMap-like index lookup via a monotonic stack. Maintain a stack of indices representing days with 'unresolved' (not yet found a warmer day) temperatures in decreasing order of temperature. For each new day, while the stack is non-empty and the current temperature is greater than the temperature at the top-of-stack index, pop the stack, compute the difference in indices (that's the wait), and store it in the answer array. Push the current index onto the stack. The key insight: the stack acts as a hash-like structure that immediately resolves waiting queries in O(1) amortized per element.
hints: Use a monotonic decreasing stack storing indices.
When a warmer temperature is found, pop and compute the difference.
followUp: What if temperatures can be negative?

title: Copy List with Random Pointer
description: A special linked list is given where each node has a next pointer and a random pointer that can point to any node in the list or null. Your task is to create a completely independent deep copy of this list — a new list where modifying the copy does not affect the original. The challenge is the random pointer: when copying a node, you may not have created the node it randomly points to yet. A HashMap elegantly solves this in two passes. First pass: iterate the original list and create a new copy node for each original node, storing the mapping (original node -> copy node) in a HashMap. Second pass: iterate again, and for each original node, set copy.next = map[original.next] and copy.random = map[original.random], using the HashMap as a translation layer between original and copy nodes.
constraints: Node.random is null or points to some node in the list.
hints: Use a HashMap to map original nodes to their copies.
First pass: create all copies. Second pass: assign next and random pointers.

youtubeVideos.title: Copy List with Random Pointer - NeetCode

relatedProblems: Clone Binary Tree With Random Pointer
followUp: Can you solve it in O(1) space by interleaving the copy nodes?

title: LRU Cache
description: Design a data structure that implements a Least Recently Used (LRU) cache with a given capacity. It must support two operations: get(key) which returns the value of the key if it exists, otherwise -1, and put(key, value) which inserts or updates the key. When the cache reaches capacity, it must evict the least recently used item before inserting a new one. Both operations must run in O(1) average time. The canonical solution combines two data structures: a HashMap for O(1) key lookup, and a doubly linked list for O(1) insertion and deletion of any node. The list maintains order of use — most recently used at the front, least recently used at the tail. Every get or put moves the accessed node to the front. When eviction is needed, remove the tail node. This problem is a masterclass in combining multiple data structures to meet strict complexity requirements.

examples.input: LRUCache(2), put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2), put(4,4), get(1), get(3), get(4)
examples.explanation: Cache of capacity 2 with LRU eviction.

constraints: At most 2 * 10^5 calls to get and put.
hints: Combine a HashMap with a doubly linked list.
HashMap gives O(1) lookup; linked list gives O(1) insertion/deletion.
Maintain a dummy head and tail to simplify boundary cases.
followUp: How would you implement LFU cache?

testCases.input: capacity=2, put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2)

title: Minimum Window Substring
description: Given two strings s and t, return the minimum length substring of s that contains every character in t (including duplicates) at least the required number of times. If no such substring exists, return an empty string. This is one of the hardest sliding window problems and is the ultimate test of the frequency-hashmap pattern. Maintain a need map (frequency of each character in t) and a window map (frequency of each character in the current window). Also maintain a 'formed' counter tracking how many unique characters in t have their required count satisfied in the window. Expand the right pointer to add characters, incrementing 'formed' when a character's window count meets its needed count. Once all characters are satisfied (formed == required), shrink the left pointer to minimize the window, updating the answer whenever a smaller valid window is found.

examples.explanation: Minimum window containing A, B, C.

constraints: s and t consist of uppercase and lowercase English letters.
hints: Use a sliding window with two frequency maps.
Track how many characters are fully satisfied (have count).
Shrink the left pointer when all characters are satisfied.
relatedProblems: Substring with Concatenation of All Words
Smallest Range Covering Elements from K Lists
followUp: What if you need all minimum windows (not just one)?

title: First Missing Positive
description: Given an unsorted integer array nums, find the smallest missing positive integer. The solution must run in O(n) time and use O(1) extra space — which means you cannot use a separate hash table. This constraint forces you to use the array itself as an implicit hash map, making it one of the most creative hashing problems. The key observation is: the answer must lie in the range [1, n+1], where n is the length of the array. So use the array indices as positions. In the first pass, for each value v where 1 <= v <= n, place v at index v-1 by swapping. After rearranging, scan the array: the first index i where nums[i] != i+1 means i+1 is the missing positive. If all positions match, return n+1. This technique — using the array itself as a hash map — is called index hashing and appears in several other problems.
hints: The answer is in range [1, n+1].
Use the array itself as a hash map: place nums[i] at index nums[i]-1.
Scan for the first index i where nums[i] != i+1.
relatedProblems: Find All Numbers Disappeared in an Array
followUp: What would change if you could use O(n) extra space?

title: LFU Cache
description: Design a data structure for a Least Frequently Used (LFU) cache. It must support get(key) which returns the value or -1, and put(key, value) which inserts or updates. When the cache is full, evict the least frequently used key. If there is a tie in frequency, evict the least recently used among those. Both operations must be O(1). This is harder than LRU because you must track both frequency and recency simultaneously. The O(1) solution requires three HashMaps: (1) key -> value, (2) key -> frequency, and (3) frequency -> ordered set of keys (a LinkedHashSet to preserve insertion order for tie-breaking). Also maintain a minFreq variable tracking the current minimum frequency. On get or put, update the key's frequency: remove it from its current frequency bucket and add it to the next. On eviction, remove the least recently used key from the minFreq bucket.

examples.input: LFUCache(2), put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2), get(3), put(4,4), get(1), get(3), get(4)
examples.explanation: LFU eviction based on access frequency.

constraints: At most 2 * 10^5 calls to get and put.
hints: Use a HashMap<key, value>, HashMap<key, frequency>, and HashMap<frequency, LinkedHashSet<key>>.
Track minFrequency to find the LFU key in O(1).
followUp: How does LFU differ from LRU in real cache performance?

testCases.input: capacity=2, put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1), put(3,3), get(2)

title: Substring with Concatenation of All Words
description: Given a string s and an array of strings words where all words have equal length, find all starting indices in s where a substring is a concatenation of all words in words in any order (each word used exactly once). For example, if words = ['foo','bar'], valid substrings are 'foobar' and 'barfoo'. The window size is always fixed: words.length * wordLen. The solution uses a word-frequency HashMap for the target words and a sliding window that steps by wordLen (not by 1 character). For each possible starting offset (0 to wordLen-1), slide the window across s in word-sized steps, maintaining a current window word-count map. When the window has the right total words, compare maps. If they match, record the start index. Remove the leftmost word when the window is full or when an invalid word is encountered. The key challenge is thinking at word granularity instead of character granularity.
hints: Use a word frequency HashMap for words.
Slide a window of size (words.length * wordLen) using steps of wordLen.
Compare the current window's word counts against the target map.

youtubeVideos.title: Substring with Concatenation of All Words - LeetCode 30

followUp: Can you optimize to O(n) using an optimized sliding window per starting offset?
